rows.cssRationale: Uses the concise brand class inside the navbar because that is the idiomatic CSS anchor for a top-level brand link.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses the brand-link class with explicit navbar ancestry so XPath stays contextual without forcing brand-text dependence.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to class renames on the brand link or navbar shell.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to navbar-brand class changes and navbar ancestry rewrites; less sensitive to brand-copy changes.

rows.cssRationale: Keeps the navbar as a short class-token selector because practitioners usually preserve the top-level navbar shell as a stable component anchor.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses descendant brand-link context inside the navbar shell so XPath stays relational without leaning on brand copy.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to class-token churn on the outer shell; less sensitive to descendant reshaping inside the navbar.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to navbar-brand context rewrites and descendant-structure changes than to simple copy edits.

rows.cssRationale: Prefers the direct form-field attribute anchor that an engineer would realistically keep for a sign-in form input.
rows.xpathRationale: Scopes the same control through auth-page context so XPath uses broader descendant structure instead of a bare form transliteration.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to name/type attribute rewrites on the control itself.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to auth-page restructuring and ancestor rewrites than to minor sibling changes.

rows.cssRationale: Keeps a short input attribute selector because the form-control field surface is the cleanest CSS expression.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses auth-page descendant context to make the XPath locator intentionally relational rather than syntax-twin CSS.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to direct control attribute changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to context moves and intermediate wrapper mutations.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the submit-button contract because CSS is strongest when the control already exposes a direct stable attribute.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses the submit control inside the auth form identified by its email/password fields, which is a stronger relational XPath pattern than button copy.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to submit-type or button-class changes; less sensitive to label wording.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to auth-form field context changes, submit-type rewrites, and ancestor-form restructuring.

rows.refactoredXpathLocator: (//a[@href="/" and ancestor::div[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " feed-toggle ")] and ancestor::li[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " nav-item ")]])[1]
rows.cssRationale: Anchors the tab through its feed-toggle container plus href because that is the shortest stable CSS expression available in the RealWorld apps.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses href together with toggle-container and nav-item ancestry so XPath stays contextual without making copy the primary differentiator.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to href rewrites or nav-link class churn.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to tab-list restructuring, href rewrites, and ancestor-context changes around the feed toggle.

rows.cssRationale: Uses local card scoping plus the preview-link surface because that is the idiomatic CSS way to target the first article CTA.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses repeated-container context plus a descendant heading predicate so XPath can exploit card-relative meaning.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to repeated-card order changes, first-item shifts, and class-token churn.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to card hierarchy reshaping and descendant predicate changes than to simple attribute swaps.

rows.refactoredXpathLocator: (//form[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " comment-form ")]//div[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " card-block ")]/textarea[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " form-control ")])[1]
rows.cssRationale: Uses the comment-form control surface directly because placeholder/name attributes are the cleanest CSS anchors for this field.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses comment-form and card-block context around the textarea control so XPath stays structural instead of placeholder-driven.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to placeholder/name attribute changes on the textarea itself.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to block-wrapper reshaping, textarea-class changes, and comment-form hierarchy mutations.

rows.refactoredXpathLocator: (//form[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " comment-form ")]//div[contains(concat(" ", normalize-space(@class), " "), " card-footer ")]//button[@type="submit" and ancestor::form[.//textarea]])[1]
rows.cssRationale: Uses footer-scoped submit-button classes because a practitioner would normally keep a short footer > button selector here.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses the footer-scoped submit control inside the form that owns the comment textarea, which is a defensible relational XPath choice.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to footer/button class churn and direct submit-surface changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to footer hierarchy mutations, submit-type changes, and comment-form context rewrites.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the textarea field contract directly because CSS is strongest on the control surface exposed by the settings form.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses settings-page form context plus the bio field contract rather than leaning on placeholder copy.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to field attribute changes on the textarea itself.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to settings-form restructuring and field-context moves than to placeholder wording changes.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the visible submit button classes because that is the concise CSS a maintainer would actually keep.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses the submit control inside the settings form anchored by the bio field, which better reflects XPath relational engineering than button copy.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to button class churn and button-type changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to settings-form restructuring, submit-type or button-class changes, and field-context rewrites.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the action button inside the article meta region because CSS is strongest on a short meta-scoped button selector.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses article-meta ancestor context so XPath captures local relational meaning instead of another global button attribute selector.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to action-button class churn and aria-label changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to meta-region restructuring, wrapper insertion, and relational context swaps.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the direct preview paragraph with its role/attribute surface because CSS reads best when it stays on the rendered content node.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses descendant note context under the preview link so XPath can express relational content lookup explicitly.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to role or attribute removal on the paragraph and to direct wrapper changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to descendant-text restructuring, wrapper insertion, and accessible-name source rewrites.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the delete control inside the comment footer because CSS is strongest on short local container scoping.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses footer-relative descendant context so XPath is intentionally more structural than the CSS form.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to direct aria-label and footer-class changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to comment-footer restructuring and nested control duplication.

rows.cssRationale: Keeps the banner heading selector short because that is the idiomatic CSS expression for the article title surface.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses banner-scoped heading structure rather than non-empty text predicates, keeping XPath contextual without making copy part of the locator.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to banner/heading class or tag changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to banner hierarchy rewrites, heading replacement, and structural wrapper changes around the title.

rows.refactoredXpathLocator: //ul[contains(@class,"pagination")]/li[.//button[@aria-label="Go to page N"]]//button[@aria-label="Go to page N"]
rows.cssRationale: Uses direct-child pagination link/button scoping because CSS is good at concise parent-child contracts for pager controls.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses list-item predicates so XPath can model the button as a child of the selected pager item rather than a flat attribute lookup.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to direct-child disruptions such as wrapper insertion or button/link surface changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to list-structure rewrites, sibling insertion, and item-level hierarchy changes.

rows.cssRationale: Uses :has()-style parent selection because CSS can express the pager item as the element containing the page control.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses a list-item predicate with the nested pager control because XPath naturally models relational container selection.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to child-control movement that invalidates the immediate parent relation.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to broader list-item restructuring and predicate-target movement.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the local user-info action button surface because this is the shortest maintainable CSS expression for the profile CTA.
rows.xpathRationale: Keeps the XPath scoped to the user-info container so it stays relational rather than another class-token transliteration.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to button class churn and aria-label changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to user-info container restructuring and inserted wrapper depth.

rows.cssRationale: Uses the local user-info action button surface because that is the idiomatic CSS anchor for the toggle control.
rows.xpathRationale: Uses the user-info container context so XPath stays relational and distinct from the CSS selector surface.
rows.cssExpectedMutationSensitivity: Most sensitive to button class churn and aria-label changes.
rows.xpathExpectedMutationSensitivity: More sensitive to user-info container restructuring and relational ambiguity.
